narrative.intro: You've landed at BER—Berlin's single airport since Tegel and Schönefeld closed. Here's the fast start: no tourist traps, no confusion, just the moves that make navigating this sprawling, cash-friendly, historically-weighted city feel natural from day one. Berlin is huge (eight times the area of Paris), so lean on the U-Bahn and S-Bahn, treat each neighborhood as its own destination, and don't try to cram it into a tidy walking radius.

arrival.title: From the Airport
arrival.content:
**BER → city:** The **FEX (Airport Express)** runs every 15–30 min to Hauptbahnhof (central station) in about 25–30 min. The **S9** is slower but runs around the clock and stops at more stations; **regional RE/RB trains** also call at BER and reach Alexanderplatz fast. All leave from the same station beneath Terminal 1 and accept the same ticket.

**Important:** BER is in fare **zone C**, so you need an **ABC ticket** (about €4.70 single, ~€11 day pass)—a plain AB city ticket is *not* valid for the airport leg. Buy it at the platform machines and **validate** before boarding.

**Taxis:** No flat rate; expect €50–70 metered to the center. Uber/Bolt are similar. The train is far cheaper and usually faster.

**Pro tip:** If you're sightseeing heavily, the **Berlin WelcomeCard (incl. zone ABC)** bundles transport with museum discounts and pays for itself quickly.

gettingAround.title: Getting Around
gettingAround.content:
**U-Bahn and S-Bahn are king.** The U-Bahn (subway) and S-Bahn (urban rail) cover everything, running roughly 4:30am–1am on weekdays and *all night* on weekends. Buy tickets at machines or the BVG app; validate them at the platform stamps. No turnstiles, but random inspections mean a €60 fine if caught without a valid, validated ticket.

**Zone AB** covers central Berlin (what you'll need day-to-day, ~€3.80 single / €9.90 day pass). **Zone C** adds the airport and Potsdam—buy ABC for those trips.

**Trams** run mostly in the former East and are handy for Prenzlauer Berg and Friedrichshain.

**Buses** fill the gaps—the 100 and 200 famously pass major sights for the price of a normal ticket.

**Bikes** are everywhere—rental stands and apps on every corner. Berlin is flat, broad, and genuinely cycle-friendly.

**Taxis/rideshare** are reasonable by European standards. Uber and Bolt work fine, and night buses (N-lines) cover gaps when the U-Bahn pauses on weeknights.

money.title: Money & Payments
money.content:
**Cash is more important here than elsewhere.** Many Berlin bars, clubs, and smaller restaurants are cash-only. "Nur Bargeld" or "Kein Kartenzahlung" mean no cards.

Keep **€50-100** on hand for going out. Cards work at larger restaurants, hotels, and shops—but don't assume.

**Tipping:** Round up or add 5-10% at restaurants. Tell the server the total you want to pay when they bring the bill. Tips at bars aren't expected but rounding up is appreciated.

**ATMs:** Plentiful. Use bank machines (Sparkasse, Deutsche Bank) to avoid fees.

connectivity.title: Staying Connected
connectivity.content:
**WiFi is standard** in cafés and hotels. Many bars have it; some clubs don't (by design). Public WiFi exists at some stations and squares but is patchy.

**EU SIMs roam free.** Non-EU visitors should grab a prepaid SIM from **O2, Vodafone, or Telekom**—available at electronics stores and some kiosks. €15-20 for solid data.

**Outlets:** Type C/F, 230V. Standard European plugs.

timing.title: Local Rhythms
timing.content:
**Berlin runs late.** Very late by most standards.

**Brunch:** 10am–3pm is the window. Weekend brunch is a religion. Many places don't open before 10am.

**Lunch:** Less of a thing; grab a döner or Asian food on the go.

**Dinner:** 7–8pm is early; 9pm is normal. Kitchens close by 10:30-11pm at most places.

**Nightlife:** Clubs open at midnight at earliest. Peak entry 1-3am. Many run until Monday morning or beyond. Don't show up before 1am.

**Shops:** 10am–8pm weekdays. **Closed Sunday** (except some in tourist areas and Hauptbahnhof).

**Spätis** (late-night shops): Open late, sell beer, are gathering spots. Essential infrastructure.

quickWins.title: Insider Moves
quickWins.content:
**Bring cash.** Berlin is not cashless. Clubs, bars, and many restaurants are "nur Bargeld."

**Sundays are quiet.** Shops are closed. Museums, parks, flea markets, and brunch are the moves.

**Club dress code is anti-dress code.** Dark, minimal, no effort showing. Definitely no suits or flashy outfits. Berghain's rejection rate is real—accept it gracefully.

**Don't photograph in clubs.** Seriously. Phone cameras get taped at doors.

**Döner after 2am** is tradition. Mustafa's is famous; Rüyam Gemüse Kebap has faster lines.

**The Reichstag dome is free** but requires advance booking online. Do it—sunset views are worth it.

**Sundays at Mauerpark.** Flea market, karaoke, people-watching. Peak Berlin.

faqs.question: Is Berlin safe for tourists?
faqs.answer: Very safe overall. Petty theft exists in tourist areas and on public transit—watch your belongings at Alexanderplatz, on the U-Bahn, and in crowded clubs. Some areas (parts of Neukölln, Wedding) feel grittier but aren't dangerous. The real risk is losing track of time in a 36-hour club session.

faqs.question: How much should I budget per day?
faqs.answer: Berlin remains affordable by Western European standards—€90-130 per person daily for mid-range comfort. Budget travelers can manage €50-70 with hostels and döner. Club entry runs €10-20 (if you get in). Beer is €3-4 at bars, meals €10-15. It's changing, but still cheaper than Paris or London.

faqs.question: Do I need to speak German?
faqs.answer: Not essential—English is widely spoken, especially in tourist areas, restaurants, and by younger people. Berlin is remarkably international. But 'Hallo,' 'Danke,' 'Bitte,' and 'Entschuldigung' (excuse me) are appreciated. Some traditional spots in outer neighborhoods have less English.

faqs.question: What's the best area to stay?
faqs.answer: Mitte is central but touristy. Kreuzberg offers nightlife and multicultural energy. Friedrichshain has clubs and student vibes. Prenzlauer Berg is calmer with cafés and families. Neukölln is edgier but increasingly hip. All are well-connected by U-Bahn/S-Bahn—stay near a station and you're fine.

faqs.question: Should I buy attraction tickets in advance?
faqs.answer: Helpful for Museum Island (especially the Pergamon), the Reichstag dome (free but requires booking), and the DDR Museum. Most other sites are walk-up friendly. Clubs don't do advance tickets—and some famously don't let everyone in regardless.

faqs.question: Is Berlin tap water safe?
faqs.answer: Absolutely—Berlin's tap water is excellent. Restaurants will bring it if you ask ('Leitungswasser, bitte'), though some push bottled. Public fountains are drinkable. The city is proud of its water quality.

faqs.question: How does the nightlife work?
faqs.answer: Clubs open late (midnight earliest) and run until Monday morning—or longer. Many have no closing time. Berghain's door policy is famously selective; dress dark, go alone or in pairs, don't take photos in line, and accept rejection gracefully. Cash only in most clubs. Pre-drinking is standard.

faqs.question: What should I definitely not miss?
faqs.answer: Beyond the obvious (Wall, Brandenburg Gate, Museum Island): the Reichstag dome at sunset (book free tickets), a döner at Mustafa's or Rüyam, the East Side Gallery, drinks at a Spätis (corner shops where locals gather), and wandering Kreuzberg on a Sunday. And yes, try the club scene—it's genuinely world-class.
